headline: Benjamin F. Potts Becomes Territorial Governor, 1870
body: Well now, cast your mind back to the year of our Lord, eighteen hundred and seventy, a time when the Montana Territory was as wild and untamed as a bucking bronco, and just as liable to throw a man clean off his senses. It was on the thirteenth day of July, a day like any other, save for the arrival of one Benjamin F. Potts, a man fresh from the battlefields and legislative halls of Ohio, sent by President Ulysses S. Grant himself to wrangle this unruly patch of earth. He stepped into the governor's boots, a pair that must have felt mighty large, considering the chaos that had been brewing in these parts. The territory, you see, was a place where law and order were often as scarce as hen's teeth, and a man's fortune could be made or lost quicker than a gambler's last dollar. Vigilante justice, they called it, a rough-and-tumble sort of law that made its own rules, and often enough, broke 'em too. Governor Potts, bless his earnest heart, set about the task of taming this wild spirit. He wasn't one for grandstanding, mind you, but a man of steady purpose, aiming to nail down some proper governance, coax a bit of economic prosperity from the stubborn soil, and, by thunder, try to make some sense of the ever-vexing Indian affairs. It was a long haul, his tenure stretching out like a Montana horizon, thirteen years in all, a considerable stretch for any man to keep his head above the swirling currents of frontier politics. He saw the need for a stable foundation, a framework of laws and administration that would allow the territory to grow, much like a sapling needing firm ground to become a mighty pine. He championed fiscal responsibility, a notion as popular as a tax collector at a gold strike, but a necessary one nonetheless. He looked at the ledgers, those dry, dusty tomes of figures, and saw a story of mounting debts, a burden on the honest folk trying to carve out a living. In his message to the Ninth Legislative Assembly in January of '76, a document that caused quite a stir, he laid it out plain as day. He spoke of the "rapid increase of the local indebtedness" and declared, "Our sources of revenue are too limited to allow so rapid an increase of local indebtedness and yet avoid the dishonor of our credit." He wasn't one to mince words, our Governor Potts, when it came to the public purse. He urged the consolidation of offices, the reduction of official fees, and even suggested taxing mining property and church edifices, ideas that likely ruffled more than a few feathers. He saw the need for a system where public officers received a fixed salary, rather than relying on fees, a practice he believed led to mischief and imbalance. He even had a notion to increase the license tax on spirituous liquors, observing with a keen eye that such libations did "more to create debt, and the necessity for a high rate of taxes, than is realized from the ordinary taxation of such properly." He understood that the cost of a good time often fell upon the shoulders of the sober, and he aimed to balance that ledger. Through all the squabbles and the struggles, the political maneuvering and the sheer grit of governing a territory on the cusp of change, Potts held firm. He was a man who believed in the steady hand of law, the promise of honest enterprise, and the slow, grinding work of building a society out of the wilderness. His efforts, though perhaps not as flashy as a gold rush or a gunfight, were the very bedrock upon which Montana would eventually rise to statehood, a testament to the quiet, persistent labor of a man who dared to bring order to the untamed West.
